objective: Diagnose a metric drop and evaluate a market entry decision under timed conditions, demonstrating mastery of RCA and strategy frameworks in an interview-realistic format.

realWorldAnchor.context: Swiggy faces two types of strategic decisions simultaneously: short-term operational fires (why did orders drop this week?) and long-term bets (should we enter grocery subscriptions?). Both require different thinking modes — RCA is a drilling-down exercise, strategy is a structuring-up exercise. Practicing both on the same company in one session builds the mental flexibility APM interviewers are looking for.

teamContext: Data and Strategy teams are most relevant today — the RCA case is what a PM does with their data analyst on a Monday morning, and the strategy case is what a PM presents to their CPO in a quarterly planning review.

tasks.title: How to diagnose a metric drop: a structured PM framework
tasks.summary: Metric drop diagnosis is the most common analytical case in APM interviews and one of the most frequent real-world PM tasks. This reading covers the structured approach: start with data integrity, then segment by dimension (platform, geography, user cohort, funnel step), form hypotheses in order of likelihood, and recommend what to check next. The key discipline is moving from observation to structured hypothesis — not jumping to a solution.
keyTakeaways: Always start a metric investigation by ruling out data collection issues — a broken analytics tag causes more 'metric drops' than actual product problems
Segment before you hypothesise: split by platform (iOS/Android/web), geography, user cohort, and funnel step — the drop usually hides in one segment
A good RCA tree has mutually exclusive branches: if 'server-side issue' and 'client-side issue' overlap, your tree is not MECE and you will investigate in circles
The output of a metric investigation is not the answer — it is the ranked list of hypotheses and the exact data you need to test each one
In an interview, the interviewer is evaluating your structure, not your conclusion — a well-structured wrong hypothesis beats an unstructured correct guess

tasks.title: Strategy cases for PMs: market entry, make vs buy, and strategic recommendation frameworks
tasks.summary: Strategy cases test whether a PM can think at the business level, not just the feature level. The most common formats are: should we enter market X, should we build or buy capability Y, and what should our strategy be in the face of competitor move Z. This reading covers the four dimensions every strategy answer must address: market attractiveness, competitive position, capability fit, and timing — and how to structure a clear recommendation that a CPO could act on.
keyTakeaways: A strategy recommendation must have a stance — 'it depends' is not an answer, it is a refusal to do the PM's job; state your recommendation in the first sentence, then justify it
Market entry cases require four lenses: how big is the market (TAM), can we win (competitive moat), do we have the capability (build/buy/partner), and is now the right time (timing and sequencing)

The most common strategy case mistake: spending 80% of the answer on market sizing and 0% on capability assessment — interviewers are specifically watching for whether you understand what your company is actually good at

Strategic recommendation format: Recommendation (1 sentence) → Market case (2-3 sentences) → Competitive case (2-3 sentences) → Capability case (2-3 sentences) → Timing (1-2 sentences) → Risks and mitigations (2-3 bullets)

For Indian company cases, always consider the regulatory dimension: any market entry into fintech, healthcare, or telecom requires RBI/IRDAI/TRAI consideration, which is a capability and timing question, not just a market size question

tasks.promptTitle: Warm-up coaching: mini RCA and mini strategy case before timed assessment
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior APM interview coach at an Indian product company.

Role: I am about to do two timed case practice sessions as my Day 19 assessment. Before I start the timer, give me a focused 30-minute warm-up — one mini RCA and one mini strategy framing — to activate the right thinking modes.

Today I studied:
- Structured RCA: data integrity → segmentation → MECE hypothesis tree → next steps
- Strategy cases: market attractiveness, competitive position, capability fit, timing framework
- Both applied to Swiggy as today's anchor company

From earlier in the programme:
- MECE framework (Day 2): mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive
- North Star + AARRR (Day 8): funnel thinking for metric drops
- Market sizing (Day 15): TAM/SAM/SOM for strategy cases

Warm-up Part 1 — Mini RCA (12 minutes)
Give me a small metric drop scenario (not Swiggy order volume — save that for my assessment). Give me ONE metric, ONE week's data, and ask me to build a quick segmentation tree. After I respond, tell me: did I start with data integrity? Did I segment before hypothesising? Was my tree MECE?

Warm-up Part 2 — Mini strategy framing (12 minutes)
Give me a one-sentence strategy question for an Indian company (not Swiggy grocery subscriptions — save that for my assessment). Ask me to state my recommendation in one sentence before structuring the answer. After I respond, tell me: did I take a stance? Did I address all four dimensions (market, competitive, capability, timing)?

Warm-up Part 3 — One coaching tip (6 minutes)
Based on my two warm-up answers, give me one specific thing to watch out for in my timed cases today.

Begin with Part 1.

bonusTasks.title: Search for Swiggy's publicly known strategic moves into non-food categories
bonusTasks.keyword: Swiggy Instamart grocery strategy Swiggy Genie instant delivery non-food expansion 2022 2023 2024
bonusTasks.why: Grounding your strategy case answer in actual documented Swiggy moves makes your recommendation more credible and India-specific — interviewers at Indian companies notice when candidates know the company's actual strategic history.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Two timed strategy and metrics cases back-to-back. Case 1 (25 min): Full RCA on a Swiggy metric drop. Case 2 (20 min): Strategic recommendation on Swiggy grocery subscriptions. Self-score against the rubric before reading Claude's feedback.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
This is a timed assessment. Start a timer for each case. Do NOT pause to look things up.

CASE 1 — Metric Drop (25 minutes)
Swiggy's total order volume dropped 20% this Monday vs last Monday. Here is the data you have:
- App sessions: flat week-on-week
- Conversion rate (session → order): down 18%
- Cart abandonment rate: up 14%
- Drop-off at payment step: up 31%
- Drop-off at address confirmation: flat
- Android app: down 28% in orders / iOS app: down 9% / Web: flat
- No marketing campaign changes this week
- A new payment gateway was deployed last Thursday

Produce:
1. A MECE RCA tree with at least 3 branches and sub-hypotheses
2. Your top hypothesis (1 sentence with justification)
3. Three specific things you would check next, and what you expect each to show if your hypothesis is correct

CASE 2 — Strategy (20 minutes)
Should Swiggy enter the grocery subscription business? (A monthly subscription where users pay a fixed fee and get unlimited free delivery on grocery orders above ₹199, plus 5% cashback on all Instamart orders.)

Structure your answer using the four dimensions:
1. Market: is there a real market for this in India? How large?
2. Competition: who else is doing this and how does Swiggy's position compare?
3. Capability: does Swiggy have what it takes to execute this?
4. Timing: is now the right time? What would change your answer?

End with a clear one-sentence recommendation.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Case 1: (1) Labelled MECE tree with at least 3 branches, (2) Top hypothesis in one sentence with justification, (3) Three numbered next steps each with what you expect to find. Case 2: Four labelled sections (Market / Competition / Capability / Timing) with 3-4 sentences each, ending with one-sentence recommendation. Total: aim for 600-800 words.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior APM interview coach at an Indian product company.

I just completed two timed cases as my Day 19 assessment:
- 25 minutes for Case 1: Swiggy order volume drop 20% RCA
- 20 minutes for Case 2: Should Swiggy enter grocery subscriptions?

I studied today:
- Structured RCA: data integrity → segmentation → MECE hypothesis tree → next steps
- Strategy cases: market attractiveness, competitive position, capability fit, timing framework
- Warm-up coaching session with mini RCA and mini strategy framing

Here is my work:
[Paste your work both case answers here]

SELF-SCORE RUBRIC (fill this out BEFORE reading my feedback):

Case 1 — Metric Drop:
- Started with data integrity check before jumping to hypotheses: 0-2 points
- RCA tree is MECE (branches don't overlap, nothing missing): 0-2 points
- Top hypothesis directly connects to the data provided (payment gateway + Android drop + payment step abandonment): 0-3 points
- Three next steps are specific and testable, not generic ('check the data'): 0-3 points
Case 1 self-score: /10

Case 2 — Strategy:
- Clear one-sentence recommendation stated (not 'it depends'): 0-2 points
- All four dimensions addressed (Market / Competition / Capability / Timing): 0-4 points
- Swiggy-specific facts used (Instamart, existing delivery infrastructure, Swiggy One existing subscription): 0-2 points
- India-specific context included (Indian consumer subscription behaviour, Zepto/Blinkit competition): 0-2 points
Case 2 self-score: /10

Now review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets — for Case 1: did the tree use the data provided, not generic reasons? Did the top hypothesis name the payment gateway deployment specifically? For Case 2: was the recommendation clear and defensible? Did the capability section address Swiggy One's existing subscription infrastructure?]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets — flag if: Case 1 tree is not MECE (branches overlap or key branch is missing like 'supply-side issue — driver availability' vs 'demand-side issue — user intent'); top hypothesis ignores the Android-specific drop which is a strong signal; next steps are vague. For Case 2: recommendation is hedged or missing; market section has no numbers; capability section does not mention Swiggy One as relevant prior capability]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things you did not consider — e.g., Case 1: the payment gateway deployed Thursday is the most likely culprit given the payment-step drop AND Android-specific impact — a new SDK often has Android-specific bugs; the correct next step is to check payment success rate by gateway provider and by Android version, not just 'check the payment step'. Case 2: Swiggy already has Swiggy One (a membership product) — the strategy question is really 'should we add a grocery-specific tier to Swiggy One', not a greenfield entry; Zepto Pass and Blinkit's subscription are already in market]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight specific to Indian product interviews — e.g., Indian APM interviewers often give you data that contains a clear signal (payment gateway deployed Thursday + Android drop + payment step abandonment = new SDK bug) and are specifically testing whether you follow the evidence or get distracted by less likely hypotheses; following the data to the obvious conclusion cleanly and quickly is valued over creative but unsupported hypotheses]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: Case 1: [X/10] | Case 2: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Fluency with Data — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]; Strategic Impact — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
